epigraph.text: Make straight paths for your feet, so that the lame may not be disabled, but rather healed.

content.text: With vectors, dot products, and cross products in hand, we can now describe the simplest geometric objects in three-dimensional space: lines and planes. A line is determined by a point and a direction; a plane is determined by a point and a normal vector. These descriptions lead to elegant equations that form the foundation for more complex geometric reasoning.

In two dimensions, lines have equations like $y = mx + b$ or $ax + by = c$. These forms do not generalize well to three dimensions. Instead, we describe lines *parametrically*: as the set of points traced out as a parameter varies.

content.title: Parametric Equations of a Line
content.content:
A line in space passing through point $P_0(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ and parallel to the direction vector $\vect{v} = \langle a, b, c \rangle$ has **parametric equations**
\[
 x = x_0 + at, \qquad y = y_0 + bt, \qquad z = z_0 + ct,
\]
where $t$ ranges over all real numbers.

In **vector form**,
\[
 \vect{r}(t) = \vect{r}_0 + t\vect{v} = \langle x_0 + at, y_0 + bt, z_0 + ct \rangle.
\]

content.title: Parametric equations of a line
content.problem: Find parametric equations for the line through $P(1, 2, -1)$ parallel to $\vect{v} = \langle 3, -1, 4 \rangle$.
content.solution:
\[
 x = 1 + 3t, \qquad y = 2 - t, \qquad z = -1 + 4t.
\]
At $t = 0$, we get the point $(1, 2, -1)$. At $t = 1$, we get $(4, 1, 3)$.

content.title: Line through two points
content.problem: Find parametric equations for the line through $A(2, -1, 3)$ and $B(5, 0, -1)$.
content.solution:
The direction vector is $\overrightarrow{AB} = \langle 3, 1, -4 \rangle$. Using $A$ as the base point:
\[
 x = 2 + 3t, \qquad y = -1 + t, \qquad z = 3 - 4t.
\]
Note: $t = 0$ gives $A$; $t = 1$ gives $B$.

content.text: If all components of the direction vector are nonzero, we can solve each parametric equation for $t$ and equate:

content.title: Symmetric Equations of a Line
content.content:
If $\vect{v} = \langle a, b, c \rangle$ with $a, b, c \neq 0$, the **symmetric equations** of the line through $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ are
\[
 \frac{x - x_0}{a} = \frac{y - y_0}{b} = \frac{z - z_0}{c}.
\]

content.text: If one component (say $c$) is zero, we write $\dfrac{x - x_0}{a} = \dfrac{y - y_0}{b}$ and $z = z_0$ separately.

content.title: Symmetric equations
content.problem: Write symmetric equations for the line through $(1, 2, 3)$ with direction $\langle 2, -3, 5 \rangle$.

content.title: Parametric Equations of a Line Segment
content.content:
The line segment from $P_0$ to $P_1$ is given by
\[
 \vect{r}(t) = (1-t)\vect{r}_0 + t\vect{r}_1 = \vect{r}_0 + t(\vect{r}_1 - \vect{r}_0), \qquad 0 \leq t \leq 1.
\]

content.text: At $t = 0$, we have $\vect{r}(0) = \vect{r}_0$; at $t = 1$, we have $\vect{r}(1) = \vect{r}_1$; at $t = 1/2$, we have the midpoint.
A plane is determined by a point on it and a vector perpendicular to it (called a **normal vector**).

content.title: Equation of a Plane
content.content:
The plane through point $P_0(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ with normal vector $\vect{n} = \langle a, b, c \rangle$ has equation
\[
 a(x - x_0) + b(y - y_0) + c(z - z_0) = 0,
\]
or equivalently,
\[
 ax + by + cz = d,
\]
where $d = ax_0 + by_0 + cz_0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content: A point $P(x, y, z)$ lies on the plane if and only if the vector $\overrightarrow{P_0P} = \langle x - x_0, y - y_0, z - z_0 \rangle$ is perpendicular to $\vect{n}$. This occurs precisely when $\vect{n} \dotp \overrightarrow{P_0P} = 0$, which gives the equation.

content.title: Equation of a plane from point and normal
content.problem: Find the equation of the plane through $(2, -1, 3)$ with normal vector $\langle 4, 2, -1 \rangle$.

content.title: Equation of a plane through three points
content.problem: Find the equation of the plane through $A(1, 0, 0)$, $B(0, 2, 0)$, and $C(0, 0, 3)$.
content.solution:
We need a normal vector, which we find via the cross product:
$$
 \overrightarrow{AB} &= \langle -1, 2, 0 \rangle \\
 \overrightarrow{AC} &= \langle -1, 0, 3 \rangle
$$
\[
 \vect{n} = \overrightarrow{AB} \crossp \overrightarrow{AC}
 = \begin{vmatrix} \vecti & \vectj & \vectk \\ -1 & 2 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 3 \end{vmatrix}
 = \langle 6, 3, 2 \rangle.
\]
Using point $A(1, 0, 0)$:
\[
 6(x - 1) + 3(y - 0) + 2(z - 0) = 0 \implies 6x + 3y + 2z = 6.
\]

content.title: Parallel and Perpendicular Planes
content.content:
Two planes are:

- **Parallel** if their normal vectors are parallel: $\vect{n}_1 = c\vect{n}_2$ for some scalar $c$.

- **Perpendicular** if their normal vectors are perpendicular: $\vect{n}_1 \dotp \vect{n}_2 = 0$.

content.title: Parallel and perpendicular planes
content.problem:
The planes $2x - y + 3z = 4$ and $4x - 2y + 6z = 1$ have normal vectors $\langle 2, -1, 3 \rangle$ and $\langle 4, -2, 6 \rangle$. Since $\langle 4, -2, 6 \rangle = 2\langle 2, -1, 3 \rangle$, the planes are parallel.

The planes $x + y + z = 1$ and $x - y = 0$ have normals $\langle 1, 1, 1 \rangle$ and $\langle 1, -1, 0 \rangle$. Since $\langle 1, 1, 1 \rangle \dotp \langle 1, -1, 0 \rangle = 1 - 1 + 0 = 0$, the planes are perpendicular.

content.title: Angle Between Planes
content.content:
The **angle** between two planes is the angle $\theta$ between their normal vectors (taking $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi/2$):
\[
 \cos\theta = \frac{|\vect{n}_1 \dotp \vect{n}_2|}{|\vect{n}_1||\vect{n}_2|}.
\]

content.text: Distance from a Point to a Plane

content.title: Distance from Point to Plane
content.content:
The distance from point $P_1(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ to the plane $ax + by + cz = d$ is
\[
 D = \frac{|ax_1 + by_1 + cz_1 - d|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $P_0(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ be any point on the plane. The distance from $P_1$ to the plane is the length of the projection of $\overrightarrow{P_0P_1}$ onto the normal $\vect{n} = \langle a, b, c \rangle$:
\[
 D = \left|\text{comp}_{\vect{n}}\overrightarrow{P_0P_1}\right|
 = \frac{|\overrightarrow{P_0P_1} \dotp \vect{n}|}{|\vect{n}|}.
\]
Computing: $\overrightarrow{P_0P_1} \dotp \vect{n} = a(x_1 - x_0) + b(y_1 - y_0) + c(z_1 - z_0) = ax_1 + by_1 + cz_1 - (ax_0 + by_0 + cz_0) = ax_1 + by_1 + cz_1 - d$.

content.title: Distance from point to plane
content.problem: Find the distance from $P(1, 2, 3)$ to the plane $2x - y + 2z = 4$.

content.text: Two non-parallel planes intersect in a line. To find this line, we can:

items: Solve the system of two plane equations for two variables in terms of a parameter
Find a point on both planes and a direction vector (the cross product of the normals)

content.title: Intersection of two planes
content.problem: Find parametric equations for the line of intersection of the planes $x + y + z = 1$ and $x - y + 2z = 3$.
content.solution:
**Method 1: Solve the system.**

Subtract the equations: $2y - z = -2$, so $z = 2y + 2$.

Let $y = t$. Then $z = 2t + 2$, and from the first equation, $x = 1 - y - z = 1 - t - (2t + 2) = -1 - 3t$.

Parametric equations: $x = -1 - 3t$, $y = t$, $z = 2 + 2t$.

**Method 2: Use cross product.**

The direction of the line is perpendicular to both normals:
\[
 \vect{v} = \langle 1, 1, 1 \rangle \crossp \langle 1, -1, 2 \rangle
 = \langle 3, -1, -2 \rangle.
\]
Setting $y = 0$ in both equations: $x + z = 1$ and $x + 2z = 3$, giving $z = 2$, $x = -1$.
So the line passes through $(-1, 0, 2)$ with direction $\langle 3, -1, -2 \rangle$:
\[
 x = -1 + 3s, \qquad y = -s, \qquad z = 2 - 2s.
\]
(This is the same line with a different parameterization.)

content.title: Augustus De Morgan (1806--1871)
content.content: Augustus De Morgan, a British mathematician, made significant contributions to logic and algebra. He formalized the study of relations and is known for De Morgan's laws in logic and set theory. De Morgan also contributed to the vector analysis of lines and planes, and he was one of the founders of the London Mathematical Society.

marginNotes.text: Teach me your way, O Lord, and lead me on a level path.
Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? Tell me, if you have understanding. Who determined its measurements—surely you know!
Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding. In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths.

marginNotes.author: Euclid
marginNotes.text: There is no royal road to geometry.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text: The eternal silence of these infinite spaces frightens me.
